1: A shadow: Job's social inversion — those of lower social standing now mock the man of honor. The NT's deepest statement of this pattern is the Passion: the Son of God is mocked by soldiers, bystanders, and crucified criminals (Matthew 27:29-44). The honor-reversal Job experiences is the structural shape of Christ's humiliation; the resurrection is the re-inversion.
2: A theme: the dismissal of the useless — those whose vitality has perished. The NT's transformation is the inclusion of the weak: 'God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong' (1 Corinthians 1:27). The very people whom the world has written off as past their vigor are the subjects of God's particular attention.
3: A theme: want and famine — the outcasts who survive on the margins. The NT's beatitude addresses this: 'Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied' (Matthew 5:6). The ones driven to barren places by deprivation are the same ones Christ declares blessed.
4: A theme: saltbush and broom root — the desperate diet of the marginalized. The NT shows Christ eating with the outcast (Luke 15:2), feeding the hungry in desolate places (Mark 6:30-44), and being sustained by his father's will 'as food' (John 4:34). The desolate place is where Christ regularly appears.
5: A revelation of God: driven out from society, shouted after like thieves — the social death of the ostracized. Christ himself was 'despised and rejected by men' (Isaiah 53:3) and crucified outside the city gate (Hebrews 13:12-13). The letter to the Hebrews draws this out as the pattern for disciples: 'let us go to him outside the camp, bearing the reproach he endured.'
6: A theme: living in gullies, holes, and rocks — the dispossessed dwelling in the earth's margins. The NT's parallel: 'foxes have holes and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (Matthew 8:20). The one who made the earth had no fixed home in it.
7: A theme: crying out among the bushes — the inarticulate lament of the destitute. The NT holds that the Spirit intercedes for us 'with groanings too deep for words' (Romans 8:26) — the Spirit takes up the voiceless cry of the suffering and brings it before God. The cry beneath the nettles is heard.
8: A theme: 'children of fools, children of men without a name' — those who carry no honorable lineage. The NT's reversal: 'to all who did receive him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God' (John 1:12). The nameless are given the name; those without lineage are adopted into the family of God.
9: A type: 'I have become their song and their byword' — the mocked one whose name is a taunt. Psalms 69:12, which the NT applies to Christ (John 15:25, Romans 15:3), says the same: 'those who sit in the gate talk about me, and the drunkards make songs about me.' Job's becoming the occasion of mockery is the same pattern the NT sees fulfilled in Christ's passion.
10: A type: 'they despise me and keep their distance; they do not hesitate to spit in my face' — the full sequence of social rejection and physical contempt. The NT fulfillment is explicit: the soldiers 'spit on him' (Matthew 27:30); he was 'despised and rejected' (Isaiah 53:3). Job's suffering here carries the structural shape of the Passion's humiliation sequence.
11: A revelation of God: 'God has loosed my cord and afflicted me' — Job attributes his social collapse to divine action. The NT's parallel is the Passion: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men' (Acts 2:23). Divine intent and human wickedness operate simultaneously in both cases.
12: A shadow: 'the mob rises up; they push aside my feet and build their roads of ruin against me' — the organized assault of enemies. The NT's image of the crowd pressing against Christ and the systematic legal apparatus that condemned him has this same character of organized, coordinated assault.
13: A shadow: 'they need no one to help them' — the self-sustaining momentum of hostility against the righteous. The opposition to Christ is similarly depicted as needing no external motivation: the chief priests, Pilate, the crowd each act from their own interests, yet together they accomplish what the NT calls the Father's eternal purpose (Acts 4:27-28).
14: A shadow: 'they rush in amid the ruin' — enemies pouring through the breach like floodwater. The Passion has this quality: once the arrest began, the cascade of betrayal, denial, trial, and crucifixion followed with a logic that no human power arrested. The disciples fled; the crowd demanded death.
15: A shadow: 'terrors are unleashed against me; my dignity is driven away like the wind, my prosperity like a cloud' — the stripping of all exterior honor. The NT's Passion strips Christ of every dignity: garments divided, crown of thorns, the charge of blasphemy, death between criminals. What Job describes as social and economic stripping, Christ undergoes completely.
16: A shadow: 'my soul is poured out within me' — the language of total interior depletion. Isaiah 53:12 uses the same 'pour out' language of Christ: 'he poured out his soul to death.' The interior desolation Job describes — not just physical but the inner pouring out of self — is the pattern the Servant's death embodies completely.
17: A revelation of God: 'my bones are racked with pain, and the pain that gnaws at me never rests' — the unrelenting physical suffering. The NT identifies Christ as the one 'acquainted with grief' (Isaiah 53:3) and who 'suffered in the flesh' (1 Peter 4:1). The incarnation means God knows what it is to have bones that ache.
18: A shadow: 'with great force my clothing is seized by my disease; it binds me at the neck' — disease that grasps and restricts. At the crucifixion, Christ's garments are taken from him by force (Matthew 27:35) — the reversal of clothing as bondage into clothing as plunder.
19: A type: 'he has thrown me into the mud; I have become like dust and ashes' — total reduction to the earth from which humanity was made (Genesis 2:7). The NT's descent language applies this to Christ: 'he was buried' (1 Corinthians 15:4) — returned to the earth before being raised from it. Job's mud is the grave's anticipation.
20: A type: 'I cry to you but you do not answer; I stand, and you only stare at me' — the unanswered cry to God. The direct NT parallel is Psalms 22:2 ('I cry by day, but you do not answer'), which Matthew 27:46 applies to the crucifixion. Job's experience of divine silence is the paradigmatic expression of the Passion's most terrifying moment.
21: A shadow: 'you have become cruel to me; with the power of your hand you oppose me' — God as the apparent adversary. At the cross, God 'did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Romans 8:32) — the divine hand that opposed the Son did so on behalf of those who deserve opposition. The apparent cruelty is the mechanism of the deepest love.
22: A revelation of God: 'you lift me up on the wind; you cause me to ride it and dissolve my substance' — being borne away by divine power toward dissolution. The NT speaks of Christ being 'lifted up' (John 3:14, Job 12:32) — the crucifixion and the ascension both use this language. The lifting that Job experiences as being dissolved, Christ transforms into the lifting that draws all people to himself.
23: A shadow: 'I know you will bring me to death, to the house appointed for all the living' — the certainty of death. Christ goes to the same house: he 'tasted death for everyone' (Hebrews 2:9), entering the house appointed for all the living so that those in him might exit it by resurrection. The certainty Job accepts becomes the certainty Christ transforms.
24: A revelation of God: 'does not one in a heap of ruins reach out his hand? does one not cry out in his disaster?' — the natural reflex of the suffering to call for help. The NT's response is that God has provided exactly this: 'for everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved' (Romans 10:13). The cry from the heap of ruins is the prayer God answers.
25: A revelation of God: 'did I not weep for those in trouble? was not my soul grieved for the poor?' — Job's compassionate identification with the suffering. The NT reveals this as the divine character embodied in Christ: 'Jesus wept' (John 11:35) over Lazarus; he had 'compassion on them' (Matthew 9:36) for the crowds. The empathy Job describes is the empathy of the incarnate God.
26: A revelation of God: 'when I looked for good, evil came; when I waited for light, darkness came' — the reversal of expectation in suffering. The disciples experienced this at Golgotha: they waited for the kingdom and got a cross. Easter is the reversal: the darkness of Friday becomes the light of Sunday, the evil absorbed becomes the good released.
27: A theme: 'my inner parts churn and do not rest' — the churning of unresolved suffering. The NT holds that present suffering produces endurance (Romans 5:3-4), not because the churning ceases but because it is working toward something. The restlessness is purposeful.
28: A shadow: 'I walk in mourning without the sun; I stand up in the assembly and cry out' — public mourning without light. The NT parallel: at the crucifixion, 'from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour' (Matthew 27:45), and Christ cries out from within it. The assembly's response to the righteous sufferer is silence or mockery; God's response is resurrection.
29: A shadow: 'I have become a brother to jackals and a companion to wild birds' — identification with wild creatures of desolation. The NT places Christ in similar wilderness company: 'he was in the wilderness forty days... and he was with the wild animals' (Mark 1:13). The tamed world that should receive the righteous has expelled them to the wild margins.
30: A revelation of God: 'my skin has turned black; my bones burn with fever' — the physical signs of disease and dying. The incarnation is the Word taking on exactly this burning, blackening, mortal flesh. 'He himself bore our griefs and carried our sorrows' (Isaiah 53:4) — the specific physiology of suffering is what God bore in the Son.
31: A shadow: 'my harp has been turned to mourning and my flute to the sound of weeping' — the transformation of musical joy into lament. The NT holds that this mourning has an eschatological reversal: 'you will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will turn into joy' (John 16:20). The harp restored is the NT's image of the new creation's worship (Revelation 5:8, Job 14:2).
